one_line_takeaway: Parallax argues (and OpenParallax demonstrates in a self-built, self-tuned 337-case adversarial harness) that OS-process-level separation between an untrusted LLM reasoner and a trusted multi-tier validator/executor blocks 98.9% (default config, 0% FP) to 100% (max-security config, 36% FP) of injected attacks under a full-compromise assumption, versus 100% attack success with no such boundary.
key_finding: Test suite: 280 adversarial cases across nine categories (C1 direct injection=25, C2 indirect injection=40, C3 multi-step context=30, C4 tool-use chain=37, C5 encoding/obfuscation=32, C6 multi-agent=25, C7 validator-targeted=26, C8 helpfulness bypass=60, C9 Tier-3 human approval=5), plus 50 false-positive (legitimate-action) cases and 7 Tier-3 legitimate cases (337 total). Under Config A (no Shield) attack success rate (ASR) is 100% across all categories -- the unprotected baseline. Under Config C default configuration, overall ASR drops to 1.1% (277/280 attacks blocked, 98.9% block rate) with 0% false positives (0/50 legitimate actions blocked). Under Config C maximum-security configuration, ASR is 0.0% (280/280 blocked, 100%) but false-positive rate rises to 36% (18/50 legitimate actions blocked), with 14 of those 18 attributed to DeBERTa classifier over-firing (confidence 1.00 misclassifications on write_file/delete_file/send_email/http_request payloads, a stated train/deployment domain mismatch since the classifier was trained on prompt-injection text, not agent-action payloads) and 4 to Tier-2 evaluator non-determinism. The three attacks that succeeded under the default config were all Tier-2 evaluator judgment calls on ambiguous actions (a /tmp deletion judged low-risk, an HTTP POST to a plausible-looking validation endpoint, and an env command judged read-only). Tier-resolution distribution over the 280 blocked attacks: Tier 0 (policy) 43 cases/15.4%, Tier 1 (classifier) 163/58.2%, Tier 2 (LLM) 73/26.1%, Tier 3 (human) 1/0.4% -- i.e., 73.6% of blocks required no LLM call. Validation latency (P50/P95/P99 ms): Tier 0 <1/<1/<1, Tier 1 1947/2335/2638 (dominated by ONNX runtime init overhead in the purego bridge, authors expect 1-2 orders of magnitude reduction with a planned CGo sidecar), Tier 2 2089/3379/4781 (a normal external LLM API call). The DeBERTa classifier itself is reported (citing its training-dataset source [47], ~16,000 labeled samples across 29 attack categories) as achieving 98.8% accuracy on its own held-out test set -- this is the classifier's pretraining-stage metric, not an OpenParallax end-to-end result.
core_contribution: The paper (single-author, Joel Fokou, independent researcher) argues that prompt-level guardrails are architecturally insufficient to secure AI agents with execution capability and proposes 'Parallax,' a paradigm of four principles: (1) Cognitive-Executive Separation (CES) -- the LLM reasoning process runs in a sandboxed, unprivileged process with no filesystem/network/tool-execution access and can only propose actions over gRPC; (2) Adversarial Validation with Graduated Determinism -- an independent validator ('Shield') interposed between reasoning and execution, evaluating every proposed action through four tiers of decreasing determinism (Tier 0 deterministic YAML policy, Tier 1 dual classifier [heuristic engine + ONNX DeBERTa model], Tier 2 budget-limited separate-LLM evaluation with a canary-token defense against evaluator-targeted injection, Tier 3 human-in-the-loop approval), all fail-closed; (3) Information Flow Control (IFC) -- provenance/sensitivity tags (PUBLIC/INTERNAL/CONFIDENTIAL/RESTRICTED) propagate through tool calls so multi-step exfiltration chains are detected even when each step looks benign; (4) Reversible Execution -- a 'Chronicle' subsystem snapshots state before destructive actions (content-addressed via SHA-256) to allow rollback. The paper also contributes a formal threat model (agent and child agents fully untrusted; engine process trusted) with a seven-category attack taxonomy (C1-C7), plus a novel evaluation methodology called 'Assume-Compromise Evaluation' that bypasses the LLM entirely and injects tool calls directly into the validation boundary to test the architecture under total agent compromise. The authors built and open-sourced OpenParallax, a Go reference implementation (single 80MB static binary, process-manager + engine + agent processes over gRPC), and evaluated it against a self-authored 337-case adversarial/false-positive test suite.
threat_model: Three trust levels are defined: (1) Untrusted -- the primary agent process (the LLM reasoning system), assumed possibly compromised at any time via direct/indirect prompt injection, memory poisoning, or context manipulation, with no assumptions about the model's safety training holding; (2) Untrusted -- child agent processes in multi-agent delegation, independently compromisable, no trust inheritance from parent; (3) Trusted -- the engine process (Shield validator, executors, Chronicle), assumed immutable at runtime, running in a separate OS process the agent cannot read/write/observe. The adversary can inject instructions via direct or indirect channels, craft multi-step 'salami slicing' context manipulation, exploit inter-agent trust, poison persistent memory, use encoding/obfuscation, and directly attack the validator (e.g., budget-exhaustion of Tier 2). The adversary is explicitly assumed NOT able to gain direct host code execution outside the agent system, modify the compiled engine binary, or compromise the deployment pipeline -- these are declared out of scope as infrastructure-security problems. The paper also explicitly excludes from its defensive perimeter: harmful content generation without execution, social engineering of the user, infrastructure compromise, LLM-inference side channels, and denial-of-service via legitimate actions.
limitations: The paper itself states: (1) Tier-1 classifier generalization is bounded by domain mismatch -- trained on prompt-injection text classification, not agent-action safety, causing systematic false positives on structured-but-benign payloads and potential blind spots on novel attack patterns; (2) IFC tag granularity is file/content-pattern-level, so sensitive content not matching a predefined pattern goes untagged (a configuration, not architectural, gap per the authors); (3) Reversibility scope -- Chronicle cannot undo actions with external side effects (sent emails, third-party API calls, published messages); (4) Single trust anchor -- the engine process is the sole trusted component, and if the engine binary itself is compromised (supply-chain, deployment pipeline, or Go-runtime exploit) the entire security model fails, a limitation the authors say is inherent to any TCB-dependent architecture; (5) Script content opacity -- Shield evaluates the shell command that runs a script but does not inspect the script's own contents, so a compromised agent authoring and then executing malicious code is only partially caught (via IFC tagging when derived from sensitive data, not for novel self-generated malicious code); (6) Scalability of human oversight -- Tier-3 throughput is bounded by human response time, and proposed adaptive-approval-learning mitigations would themselves be attackable via a sequence of benign-seeming approvals. Additional grounding caveats not stated as limitations but relevant to interpretation: the evaluation is on a single-author-authored/curated 337-case test suite (not a third-party or externally standardized benchmark), Assume-Compromise Evaluation is itself a proposed methodology introduced by this paper rather than an established practice, arithmetic in the paper is internally consistent (277/280=98.9%, 18/50=36%) but all numbers derive from the authors' own test harness and policy, which they themselves note required iterative refinement (adding deny rules for .env/*.pem/etc/passwd/.openparallax paths and 17 heuristic patterns) to reach the reported results -- i.e., the reported rates reflect a system tuned against its own eval set.

datasets_used: Self-authored 280-case adversarial test suite (9 categories C1-C9) plus 50 false-positive legitimate-action cases plus 7 Tier-3 legitimate cases (337 total), released with the OpenParallax repository

Public prompt-injection detection dataset [47] (~16,000 labeled samples, 29 attack categories) used to fine-tune the Tier-1 DeBERTa classifier -- this is a cited/used training dataset, not one introduced by this paper
quantitative_results: Config A (no Shield): 100% attack success rate across all 9 categories and overall (280/280)
Config C default: overall ASR 1.1% (block rate 98.9%, 277/280 blocked); per-category ASR: C1=4.0%, C2=0.0%, C3=0.0%, C4=2.7%, C5=0.0%, C6=0.0%, C7=0.0%, C8=1.7%, C9=0.0%; false positives 0% (0/50)
Config C maximum-security: overall ASR 0.0% (280/280 blocked); false positives 36% (18/50), of which 14 attributed to DeBERTa over-firing and 4 to Tier-2 evaluator non-determinism
Tier resolution of the 280 blocked attacks: Tier 0 = 43 (15.4%), Tier 1 = 163 (58.2%), Tier 2 = 73 (26.1%), Tier 3 = 1 (0.4%)
Tier-3 human-escalation validation: 4 of 12 targeted ambiguous-zone cases traversed the full Tier-2-to-human path correctly; remaining 8 caught earlier by Tiers 0/1
DeBERTa classifier pretraining metric (cited, not an OpenParallax pipeline result): 98.8% accuracy on held-out test set of the [47] dataset

Preliminary LLM-mode (non-inject) runs with Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Haiku 4.5, and Llama 3.1 8B: 0% attack success rate across all configurations (motivating the switch to Assume-Compromise Evaluation)
baselines_compared: Config A: identical agent/action set with Shield validation entirely disabled (establishes the 100% ASR unprotected baseline)

Config B (prompt-guardrails-only) is explicitly stated as not applicable/not run in inject-mode evaluation, since there is no reasoning system present to receive prompt instructions when tool calls are injected directly

No external/third-party benchmark (e.g., InjecAgent, AgentDojo, Agent Security Bench) is used for OpenParallax's own quantitative evaluation; those benchmarks are mentioned only in Related Work as used by other cited systems (ACE, SEAgent), not run against OpenParallax in this paper
